organization_purpose.purpose_of_the_orga.answer:
Riverbend Youth & Family Services is a mid-sized, community-based nonprofit founded in 2011 in the north side neighborhoods of Dayton, Ohio. Our primary purpose is to support young people ages 8–21 and their families who are facing a combination of economic hardship, academic challenges, and exposure to community violence.

We operate in three core program areas:

1) **After-School Academic Support and Enrichment**  
We run a free, five-days-a-week after-school program at our Willow Creek Community Center from 3:00–7:00 PM during the school year. We partner with three local public schools (Riverbend Middle School, North Dayton High School, and Lincoln Elementary) to identify students who are reading or doing math below grade level, or who have high absenteeism or behavioral referrals.

- Each youth is paired with a volunteer tutor or staff member who helps them complete homework, practice literacy and math skills, and set weekly academic goals.  
- We integrate enrichment activities such as art, music, coding clubs, and recreational sports to keep youth engaged. For example, our Tuesday “STEM Lab” is run by volunteer engineering students from the local community college; they help middle-schoolers build simple robots and learn basic coding using Scratch.  
- We provide a healthy snack and a light dinner each day. For many of our participants, this is their most reliable evening meal, which we know because parents often tell us they plan their grocery shopping around our program schedule.

We measure impact by tracking school attendance, grades, and teacher feedback (with parental consent). Over the past two school years, 71% of our regularly attending students improved at least one letter grade in math or reading, and 64% reduced unexcused absences.

2) **Family Stability & Resource Navigation**  
We recognize that young people thrive when their caregivers are supported, so we offer practical assistance and coaching to families:

- A part-time family resource navigator meets one-on-one with parents and caregivers to help them access rental assistance, utility support, SNAP benefits, and low-cost or free mental health services. For example, last winter we helped a single father of three, who had recently been laid off from a warehouse job, secure emergency rental assistance and connect with a job training program at Goodwill Easter Seals.  
- We host monthly workshops on topics like budgeting on a limited income, supporting children with ADHD or learning differences, and navigating IEP/504 plans in schools. These workshops are often co-facilitated by local professionals (e.g., a school psychologist from Dayton Public Schools or a representative from Legal Aid of Western Ohio).
- We maintain a small emergency essentials pantry (diapers, hygiene items, basic school supplies, and winter accessories like hats and gloves) that families can access once per month. We intentionally keep the process low-barrier—no lengthy forms, just a simple sign-in sheet—because we want families to feel respected and not shamed for needing help.

3) **Youth Leadership, Employment Readiness, and Mental Wellness**  
For teens and older youth (ages 14–21), we run a leadership and employment readiness track:

- Our “Leaders in the Bend” program runs year-round and includes weekly small-group sessions focused on communication skills, conflict resolution, decision-making, and goal-setting. Youth plan and lead at least one community service project per year; last summer they organized a neighborhood clean-up and mural painting along Willow Creek Avenue, working with a local artist to design a mural that reflects the history and diversity of the neighborhood.  
- We offer job readiness workshops, including resume writing, interview practice (mock interviews with volunteer HR professionals), and basic workplace expectations. We also connect youth to summer jobs and internships through the county’s youth employment program and local businesses (e.g., placements at a community garden, a senior center, and a small printing shop).  
- Because many of our youth have experienced trauma—whether from community violence, housing instability, or family separation—we integrate mental wellness supports. A licensed social worker from a partnering agency provides on-site small-group sessions on stress management and emotional regulation, and offers short-term individual counseling slots one afternoon per week. We have seen youth who initially refused to talk later request one-on-one time with the social worker after building trust in the group setting.

**Who We Serve:**  
Approximately 85% of the youth we serve are from low-income households, with most families living at or below 150% of the federal poverty line. The majority are Black or African American, with growing representation from Latino and immigrant families. Many households are headed by single parents or grandparents; several families are navigating reentry after incarceration, domestic violence, or recent homelessness.

We prioritize youth who are:
- At risk of academic failure or school disengagement (chronic absenteeism, suspensions, or failing core subjects).  
- Living in households experiencing housing instability or food insecurity.  
- Impacted by community violence, either directly (victims or witnesses) or indirectly (loss of family members or friends).  
- First-generation high school or college graduates in their family.

**How We Operate and Our Values:**  
Our approach is relational and strengths-based. Many of our staff and volunteers grew up in Dayton or in similar neighborhoods and share cultural and lived experiences with the youth we serve. We invest heavily in building trust—showing up consistently, following through on what we promise, and involving youth and parents in decision-making.

We maintain a Youth Advisory Council comprised of 10 high school students who meet monthly to give feedback on programs, propose new ideas, and help us identify what supports are most relevant. For example, it was the Youth Advisory Council that pushed us to add a “College & Careers Night” twice a year, where we now invite representatives from community colleges, trade programs, and local employers. Parents also have a Parent Advisory Circle that meets quarterly to discuss what is working well, what needs improvement (for instance, they asked us to extend our hours to 7:00 PM to better accommodate work schedules), and to suggest new workshops.

**Why Our Work Matters:**  
Our neighborhood has seen years of disinvestment; families talk about factories closing, increasing rents, and fewer safe spaces for kids to gather. Before Riverbend Youth & Family Services opened, there was no consistent, low-cost after-school program within walking distance for many of our families. We have heard from both parents and school staff that when youth are with us after school, they are safer, more supported, and more likely to stay on track academically. One parent recently shared that her son used to come home and play video games alone until late at night; now he comes to our program, gets his homework done, plays basketball with friends, and is in bed by 10 PM.

In practical terms, our organization exists to:
- Provide a safe, structured environment during out-of-school hours.  
- Improve academic outcomes for youth who are at risk of falling behind.  
- Strengthen family resilience by connecting caregivers to resources and practical tools.  
- Promote mental wellness, positive identity, and leadership among young people who are often underestimated or overlooked.  

We do this through consistent programming, strong community partnerships (with schools, faith communities, and other nonprofits), and a commitment to listening to and elevating the voices of the youth and families we serve.

support_requested.other.answer:
In addition to financial and volunteer support, we are seeking in-kind contributions and capacity-building assistance that will help us sustain and deepen our impact over the next 12–24 months.

**1) Financial Support – How Funds Will Be Used**  
We are requesting financial support primarily to stabilize and expand our after-school and youth leadership programs. Specifically, grant funds from the Big Wheel Community Foundation would be allocated in the following ways:

- **Program Staff Hours (After-School & Youth Leadership):** Our current funding only covers 20 hours per week for our Program Coordinator and 10 hours per week for our part-time Youth Mentor. As enrollment has grown from an average of 28 youth per day in 2021–2022 to 41 youth per day in 2024–2025, we are struggling to maintain a safe staff-to-youth ratio and to provide individualized academic support. Additional funding would allow us to increase the Program Coordinator’s hours to full-time (including planning time) and add another part-time Youth Mentor. This means more consistent supervision, more small-group tutoring, and more intentional relationship-building with youth and families.

- **Healthy Meals and Snacks:** A significant portion of our budget goes toward providing a daily snack and light dinner for youth. While we receive some donated items from the local food bank, we still purchase fresh fruit, milk, and protein options weekly. In the past year, food costs have risen substantially, and we have had to make difficult choices (e.g., reducing fresh fruit offerings from three days per week to two). Financial support would ensure that we can offer a nutritious snack and meal five days a week without cutting corners.

- **Program Materials and Supplies:** This includes school supplies (notebooks, pencils, calculators), art supplies (paint, canvases, craft materials), STEM materials (robotics kits, basic tablets for coding activities), and sports equipment (basketballs, cones, jump ropes). For example, our current robotics kits are shared among too many students, which means not every youth gets hands-on time in each session. Additional funding would allow us to purchase 2–3 more kits so youth can work in smaller groups.

- **Transportation Support:** While we are not yet able to operate a full shuttle service, we do provide bus passes to families who live further away or who have inconsistent access to transportation. We have had to limit the number of bus passes we offer each month due to cost. Funding would enable us to increase the number of youth who can attend regularly by reducing transportation barriers.

**2) Volunteer Support – How Volunteers Will Be Engaged**  
We are actively seeking volunteers in several key roles, and support from the Big Wheel Community Foundation’s network could be transformative:

- **Academic Tutors and Homework Helpers:** We need consistent volunteers (ideally committing to at least one regular afternoon per week) who can assist youth with reading, writing, math, and science homework. Volunteers do not need to be certified teachers, but we do look for individuals who are patient, reliable, and comfortable working with middle- and high-school students. For example, we currently have a retired math teacher who comes on Mondays and has become a huge asset to several 9th graders struggling with algebra.

- **Career & Life Skills Mentors:** We would love to connect youth with volunteers who can share real-world experience in fields like healthcare, skilled trades, technology, entrepreneurship, and public service. These volunteers might lead a short workshop (e.g., “What It’s Like to Be a Nurse” or “How I Started My Small Business”) or host a small group conversation about career paths. We have found that youth respond strongly when they can see concrete examples of people from similar backgrounds succeeding in different fields.

- **Special Event and Logistics Volunteers:** We host several events throughout the year—family nights, community clean-ups, and our annual Back-to-School Resource Fair. Volunteers help with set-up, registration, distributing supplies, serving food, and clean-up. This kind of support reduces the strain on our small staff and allows us to host events that feel welcoming and well-organized.

We have clear processes in place for screening, orienting, and supporting volunteers (including background checks for those working directly with youth, basic training on trauma-informed engagement, and clear role descriptions), so we are prepared to effectively utilize additional volunteer support.

**3) Other Types of Support Requested**  
Beyond direct funding and volunteers, there are several additional types of assistance that would be extremely valuable:

- **Capacity-Building and Organizational Development:** We are in the process of maturing from a small, founder-led nonprofit to a more structured organization with stronger systems. Pro bono or subsidized support in strategic planning, board development, and outcome evaluation would help us ensure that we are sustainable and accountable as we grow. For example, we would benefit from coaching on developing a 3–5 year strategic plan, creating a more formalized board recruitment process, and refining our data collection tools to better track program outcomes.

- **In-Kind Donations of Technology and Equipment:** Our computer lab currently has eight functioning laptops for youth, many of which are refurbished and several years old. Additional or updated computers, a reliable projector, and a color printer/scanner would greatly improve our ability to run homework help, coding clubs, and college/career readiness workshops (e.g., helping seniors complete FAFSA forms or job applications online).

- **Connections to Mental Health and Wellness Providers:** While we have one social worker partner who offers limited on-site counseling hours, the demand far exceeds what we can currently provide. Introductions to additional mental health professionals or agencies willing to collaborate (through on-site groups, telehealth sessions, or training for our staff) would help us better address the emotional and mental health needs of our youth.

- **Publicity and Storytelling Support:** As a smaller organization, we often struggle to share our story beyond our immediate neighborhood. Guidance or assistance in building a stronger online presence, creating simple impact videos, or connecting with local media would help us reach more potential supporters and volunteers. Even something as simple as a workshop on social media strategy for nonprofits could make a difference.

In summary, while our most immediate needs are financial support to sustain and expand programming and volunteer support to deepen our academic and mentoring activities, we are also very open to in-kind, capacity-building, and partnership opportunities. We see this application not just as a request for a one-time grant, but as an invitation to build a long-term relationship with the Big Wheel Community Foundation and its broader network of partners.
